preFix.title: Macramé bottle holder
preFix.subtitle: A spiral-knotted holder for a standard 750 ml wine bottle in 4 mm single-twist cotton cord
preFix.excerpt: Uses the spiral (half-square) knot, a series of left-half square knots that rotate automatically as they stack, to form a snug tube around a 750 ml bottle. 8 cords mounted on a small ring at the bottle base, worked upward in spiral knots, finished with a hanging loop.
preFix.sourceNotes: Macramé bottle holders using the spiral knot are a widely taught introductory project in macramé instruction. Format, cord calculations, and construction sequence are original. Not drawn from a specific published source.

content.text: This bottle holder wraps a standard 750 ml wine bottle in a column of
spiral knots

, a series of left-half square knots that automatically rotate as they stack, forming a snug, twisted tube. The cord is 4 mm single-twist cotton macramé cord. Eight cords are mounted on a small metal ring at the base of the bottle using
lark's head knots
; four become working cords and four become
filler cords
. The holder is worked upward from the base ring, expanding slightly at the bottle shoulder, then consolidated at the neck with a
gathering knot
and finished with a hanging loop. This is a good first macramé project: the spiral knot requires only one repeated action.

items.name: 4 mm single-twist cotton macramé cord — 8 cords, each cut to 2 m, giving 1 m working length when folded double on the ring
Small metal ring, 3 to 4 cm diameter — for the base mount
750 ml wine bottle (or similar) — used as a form while knotting

content.text: Cord cutting

Cut 8 lengths of 4 mm single-twist cotton macramé cord, each 2 m long. When folded in half and mounted on the base ring, each cord contributes two tails of 1 m. With 8 cords mounted, you have 16 tails: arrange these into two groups of 8, one group for each of the two spiral columns that run up opposite sides of the bottle. Within each group of 8, the outer 2 tails are working cords and the inner 4 are filler cords.
Setting up

Fold each of the 8 cords in half and mount them onto the small metal ring with lark's head knots, spacing them evenly around the ring. The ring itself sits under the base of the bottle during knotting. Use T-pins to hold the ring to the macramé board so it doesn't rotate. Stand the bottle on the ring to confirm the fit: the ring should sit flat under the base without the bottle tilting.

Divide the 16 tails into two groups of 8 on opposite sides of the ring. Each group of 8 is worked as one spiral column. Place the bottle in the centre so the tails hang on two sides. You will knot one side, rotate the bottle, then knot the other.
The pattern

definition.caption: Spiral knot (left half-square knot): only the left crossing is used each time, causing the column to rotate

content.text: Knotting the first spiral column

Working with one group of 8 tails, take the outermost 2 tails as working cords and hold the inner 4 as filler cords. Tie a left-half square knot: bring the left working cord over the 4 filler cords and under the right working cord; then bring the right working cord under the 4 filler cords and up through the loop on the left. Pull snug. This is one spiral knot. Tie another left-half square knot immediately below the first, always starting from the left side. Do not alternate to a right-half knot; using only the left half is what causes the column to spiral. Continue tying spiral knots until the column reaches the bottle shoulder, approximately 20 cm above the base ring.
Knotting the second spiral column

Rotate the bottle 180 degrees to bring the second group of tails to the front. Repeat the spiral knot sequence for this group, working to the same height as the first column. Both columns should reach the shoulder at the same level.
Joining at the shoulder

At the shoulder level, bring 4 tails from the left column and 4 tails from the right column together and tie one square knot across all 8. This joins both columns and holds the holder around the bottle neck. Repeat on the opposite side with the remaining 4 tails from each column.
Finishing

Gather all 16 tails above the shoulder square knots into a single bundle. Using a separate 40 cm length of 4 mm cord, tie a gathering knot around the entire bundle: hold the short cord folded in half with the loop pointing upward; wind the long tail of the short cord tightly around the bundle 8 to 10 times, working downward; thread the winding tail through the bottom of the loop; pull the loop closed by pulling the top tail of the short cord upward through the wraps. Trim the tails of the gathering cord flush.

Divide the remaining 16 tails into 8 pairs. Braid or knot them in two groups of 8 to form a hanging loop of the required length above the gathering knot. A length of 15 to 20 cm is sufficient for a wall hook or door knob. Finish the top of the loop with a second gathering knot, then trim the cord ends to 3 cm.

items.fix: Always begin each knot from the left working cord. If unsure which cord is which, place a T-pin alongside the left working cord as a reference. Undo the last few knots and restart with only the left-half crossing.
items.cause: Alternating between left-half and right-half knots rather than repeating only the left-half knot
items.problem: The column does not spiral; it lies flat like a chain

items.fix: Work with the bottle inside the holder throughout knotting and pull each knot moderately snug against the bottle's surface. If the ring is oversized, wrap the base ring joint with a few turns of extra cord before mounting.
items.cause: The base ring is too large for the bottle base, or the knot columns are too loose
items.problem: The holder slides up the bottle and won't stay in place

items.fix: Count knots per column as you work. Match the count before joining at the shoulder.
items.cause: Different knot tension or different number of knots in each column
items.problem: The two columns are at different heights when they reach the shoulder

items.fix: Increase the wrap count to 12. Pull the locking tail firmly through all wraps so the inner loop closes tightly around the wrapping coils and cannot loosen.
items.cause: Insufficient wraps or the locking pull not executed correctly
items.problem: The gathering knot slides off the cord bundle
